HomePage.title: CCUse
HomePage.slogan: One local endpoint that takes over multi-provider failover.
HomePage.description: Point Cursor, Claude Desktop, and OpenAI-compatible tools at a loopback API. CCUse chooses a healthy provider when each request arrives and switches to a backup route when the primary chain fails.

heroPreview.label: CCUse product preview
heroPreview.caption: A CCUse local proxy monitor window showing the local endpoint, request path, and provider status.
heroPreview.failoverText: When the primary provider slows down or fails, the request moves to the next available route.

HomePage.featuresTitle: Turn many AI providers into one reliable entry point.
HomePage.featuresDescription: From local auth and health checks to strategy selection, CCUse keeps resilience inside the proxy layer while clients keep using familiar OpenAI-compatible requests.

failover.title: Seamless failover
failover.description: When the primary provider times out, rate limits, or returns a 5xx, the proxy chooses the next available route within the request chain.

multiProvider.title: Multi-provider
multiProvider.description: Manage OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, relay services, and custom endpoints behind one local Base URL for every client.

healthCheck.title: Health checks
healthCheck.description: Background probes use model listings or low-cost requests, then cache Healthy, Degraded, and Down states for fast routing decisions.

smartStrategy.title: Smart strategy
smartStrategy.description: Route by priority, speed, cost, load balancing, or weighted scoring to match stability and budget goals.

monitoring.title: Live monitoring
monitoring.description: Track request volume, latency, success rate, switch history, and estimated cost without digging through raw logs.

crossPlatform.title: Cross-platform
crossPlatform.description: Built with Tauri 2 for macOS Apple Silicon, macOS Intel, and Windows x64 desktop installers.

architecture.title: Clients stay unchanged while resilience lives in the local proxy.
architecture.description: The homepage reuses the Mermaid architecture source from the product technical document: clients point at one local endpoint, while CCUse handles auth, parsing, provider selection, forwarding, and recovery inside the Rust proxy.
architecture.mobileAlt: CCUse architecture diagram: clients connect to the local proxy, which routes requests to multiple providers.

flowDescriptions.request: Clients keep sending OpenAI-compatible requests to the local Base URL.
flowDescriptions.selection: The proxy validates the local key, parses the request, and chooses a healthy provider by the active strategy.
flowDescriptions.recovery: Failed responses trigger automatic switching while the health checker keeps provider state fresh.

metadata.title: Features
metadata.description: A detailed look at CCUse multi-provider routing, health checks, smart strategies, monitoring, and desktop delivery.

hero.title: The local proxy layer for resilient AI clients.
hero.description: CCUse gives every OpenAI-compatible client one loopback Base URL and local API Key, then handles provider selection, retries, health state, and monitoring inside the desktop app.

preview.label: CCUse feature routing preview
preview.caption: A screenshot-style preview of CCUse routing requests from local clients to healthy providers.

flow.client: Cursor / Claude Desktop sends an OpenAI-compatible request
flow.proxy: CCUse validates sk-local key and selects the active route
flow.provider: Healthy provider receives the converted request

overview.title: Each feature owns a visible piece of the proxy runtime.

failover.title: Seamless failover
failover.summary: Move a failed request to the next viable provider without changing the client.
failover.description: The proxy treats provider errors, timeouts, rate limits, and quota exhaustion as routing signals. A request starts with the active strategy, then moves to a backup provider when the current route cannot complete safely.

first.label: Triggers
first.value: Timeouts, 429, 5xx, exhausted quota, and degraded health

second.label: Boundary
second.value: Switches before a final response; streaming responses stay on one route

third.label: Client impact
third.value: Cursor, Claude Desktop, and CLI tools keep one Base URL

setup.title: Primary provider stalls
setup.caption: The request enters through the local endpoint and the active provider crosses the timeout or error threshold.
setup.footer: The failure is recorded with the provider id, status, and request context for the switch history.

routing.title: Backup route selected
routing.caption: The engine filters unhealthy providers and selects the next eligible route using the configured strategy.
routing.footer: Provider-specific converters keep the upstream request shape correct while the client still speaks OpenAI-compatible HTTP.

result.title: Client receives a normal response
result.caption: The response returns through the same local endpoint, so the calling app does not need provider-specific fallback logic.
result.footer: The switch event stays visible in monitoring so the operator can fix the failed provider later.

multiProvider.title: Multi-provider
multiProvider.summary: Put OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, relay services, and custom endpoints behind one local API.
multiProvider.description: CCUse stores provider credentials locally, lets each route define its own Base URL and priority, and exposes one local key to downstream tools. The client stays stable while the upstream provider set changes.

first.label: Provider types
first.value: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, domestic relays, and custom endpoints

second.label: Credential model
second.value: Upstream API keys stay encrypted in the desktop app

third.label: Client contract
third.value: One local Base URL and one sk-local key

setup.title: Register every upstream
setup.caption: Each provider card keeps its API Key, Base URL, model family, priority, and optional limits together.
setup.footer: Operators can add or disable providers without asking every client to update configuration.

routing.title: Convert provider formats
routing.caption: Requests arrive as a unified shape, then provider adapters translate them for each upstream API.
routing.footer: The conversion layer is what lets one local endpoint safely front providers with different native protocols.

result.title: One endpoint for all tools
result.caption: Cursor, Claude Desktop, Continue, and scripts all point at the same loopback server.
result.footer: Client configuration becomes durable even when upstream vendors, relays, or priorities change.

healthCheck.title: Health checks
healthCheck.summary: Continuously score provider availability before a user request needs a route.
healthCheck.description: Background probes keep provider status fresh using model-list checks or low-cost requests. The switch engine can avoid routes already marked Degraded or Down instead of discovering failure only on live traffic.

second.label: Signals
second.value: Latency, status code, rate limit, and recovery events

third.label: Usage
third.value: Cached status feeds routing, dashboard cards, and notifications

setup.title: Low-cost checks run in the background
setup.caption: The health checker periodically verifies whether each configured provider can accept traffic.
setup.footer: Checks are designed to be cheap enough to run continuously without becoming the main source of cost.

routing.title: Provider state is cached
routing.caption: The latest probe result is normalized into a small set of states the routing layer can read quickly.
routing.footer: A stale or failing provider can be bypassed before it slows down the next user request.

result.title: State changes surface immediately
result.caption: When a provider changes status, the app emits an event that can update the dashboard and desktop notifications.
result.footer: Health changes are visible without tailing logs or waiting for user-facing failures.

smartStrategy.title: Smart strategy
smartStrategy.summary: Choose routes by priority, latency, cost, load balancing, or weighted scoring.
smartStrategy.description: Routing can be simple and predictable or tuned for speed and cost. Smart mode combines availability, latency, cost, and priority so the best route can change as provider conditions change.

first.label: Modes
first.value: priority, fastest, cost, load_balance, and smart

third.label: Control
third.value: Operators can trade stability, speed, and budget explicitly

setup.title: Pick the routing mode
setup.caption: Use priority for deterministic order, fastest for latency, cost for budget, load balance for distribution, or smart for mixed goals.
setup.footer: Strategy is a runtime policy, not a client setting, so it can change without reconfiguring downstream tools.

routing.title: Score healthy candidates
routing.caption: Smart mode weighs availability first, then latency, cost, and priority to rank eligible providers.
routing.footer: The engine skips providers that fail health gates before comparing scores.

result.title: Best candidate wins this request
result.caption: The selected provider can differ from the previous request when latency, health, or cost metadata changes.
result.footer: Switch history records why routing moved, which keeps policy behavior explainable.

monitoring.title: Live monitoring
monitoring.summary: See provider status, latency, success rate, cost, and switch history in one dashboard.
monitoring.description: The monitoring surface turns proxy behavior into readable operations data: current provider, request totals, 24-hour success rate, latency, cost, and recent switches.

first.label: Dashboard
first.value: Provider cards, request volume, success rate, and latency

second.label: History
second.value: Newest 50 switch events with reason and provider ids

third.label: Alerts
third.value: Desktop notifications for failures, quota, and recovery

setup.title: Status cards stay current
setup.caption: The dashboard highlights the active provider and the latest health state for every route.
setup.footer: The first screen answers whether traffic is healthy before the operator opens raw logs.

routing.title: Switch history explains movement
routing.caption: Recent switch events show where traffic moved and which failure condition caused the reroute.
routing.footer: The timeline is intentionally bounded to the newest events so diagnosis stays fast.

result.title: Cost and latency trends stay visible
result.caption: Aggregate cards show whether the current strategy is meeting reliability and budget goals.
result.footer: Operators can tune routing strategy from observed behavior instead of guessing.

crossPlatform.title: Cross-platform
crossPlatform.summary: Ship the same local proxy experience to macOS Apple Silicon, macOS Intel, and Windows x64.
crossPlatform.description: CCUse is a Tauri 2 desktop app: Rust owns the local proxy runtime while React renders the control surface. The release pipeline produces the three supported installer targets only.

first.label: macOS
first.value: Apple Silicon and Intel DMG packages

second.label: Windows
second.value: x64 setup installer with WebView2 guidance

third.label: Runtime
third.value: Native tray, local HTTP server, and encrypted config

setup.title: Download the matching package
setup.caption: The website and release flow keep the supported platform artifacts explicit.
setup.footer: Unsupported bundle targets stay out of the release surface to keep downloads unambiguous.

routing.title: Native app owns the proxy
routing.caption: The desktop shell starts the local server, exposes tray actions, and keeps the proxy available while clients run.
routing.footer: The proxy stays local to the machine, which keeps client setup simple and provider secrets off remote dashboards.

result.title: Same workflow across machines
result.caption: Operators get the same provider setup, switching controls, monitoring, and local key model on every supported platform.
result.footer: Cross-platform delivery does not change the client contract: one local Base URL and one local API key.

metadata.title: Download
metadata.description: Download the latest CCUse desktop release from GitHub release assets.

hero.title: Install the local proxy on your machine.
hero.description: The download page reads the latest GitHub Release with a 60 second revalidation window, then lists the assets published by the release workflow.

release.unavailableDescription: CCUse could not read the latest GitHub Release right now. The page will retry when the ISR cache revalidates.

platform.title: Recommended package
platform.description: The page checks the browser user agent and high-entropy architecture hints when available, then highlights the matching desktop package.
platform.unknownDescription: This browser did not expose enough platform details to choose safely. Pick the package that matches your operating system and CPU.
platform.noAsset: The latest release does not include a matching package yet. Use the raw asset list below or open GitHub releases.

macosAarch64.title: macOS Apple Silicon
macosAarch64.description: For M1, M2, M3, M4, and newer Apple Silicon Macs.

macosX64.title: macOS Intel
macosX64.description: For Intel-based Macs that need the x64 DMG.

windowsX64.title: Windows x64
windowsX64.description: For 64-bit Windows desktops using the setup installer.

assets.title: Files from the latest release.
assets.description: This list is intentionally raw for now: the next tasks add platform detection, strict package cards, SHA-256 display, and pre-release separation.
assets.empty: The latest release does not expose downloadable assets yet.
assets.unavailableDescription: The GitHub API request failed or returned an unexpected response. Use the GitHub releases link while the cache refreshes.

Metadata.description: Local API proxy for multi-provider AI routing, health checks, and automatic failover.

Footer.tagline: A local API proxy for resilient multi-provider AI clients.
